description: You've already built a transformer from scratch and trained it (dynamo) — model training/architecture is done. This roadmap skips that entirely and moves to production AI engineering: deployment, LLM APIs, prompting, embeddings/vector DBs, RAG, and fine-tuning vs prompting tradeoffs. You've also already touched FAISS (semantic image search) and Groq (multi-agent tool), so those sections build on real prior work rather than starting cold.

topics.title: Model Deployment (TorchServe, FastAPI)
topics.note: You already serve dynamo via Django SSE — this generalizes that into proper ML-serving infra.

subtopics.title: TorchServe fundamentals: model archiving (.mar files), handlers, batching config
Package dynamo as a TorchServe model and compare cold-start/latency/throughput vs your current Django serving
Dynamic batching strategies for inference under concurrent load (grouping requests without breaking streaming)
resources: Triton Inference Server docs for comparison

subtopics.title: GPU memory management in production: model quantization (int8/fp16) to fit more concurrent requests on a T4-class GPU
Model versioning & rollback strategy (serving dynamo-v1 vs dynamo-v2 side by side, canary rollout)
Monitoring inference in production: latency percentiles, token/sec throughput, GPU utilization dashboards

topics.title: LLM APIs (Groq, Mistral, OpenAI-like)
topics.note: You already use Groq's free tier for your multi-agent tool — this deepens that and adds the broader API landscape.

subtopics.title: Groq API deep dive: function/tool calling, streaming, model selection tradeoffs (speed vs quality across their hosted models)
OpenAI-compatible API conventions (most providers mirror this) — request/response schema, streaming SSE format
Mistral API + open-weight model hosting options (comparing hosted APIs vs self-hosting dynamo-scale models)
Multi-provider abstraction layer (LiteLLM or hand-rolled) so your agent tool isn't locked to one provider
Cost/latency/rate-limit tradeoffs across providers — build a decision matrix for your multi-agent tool's model choice
Core techniques: few-shot, chain-of-thought, structured output prompting (JSON mode)
Tool-use/function-calling prompt design for agents — critical for your Groq multi-agent tool's reliability
System prompt design for multi-agent roles (reviewer agent vs coder agent vs planner agent)
Prompt evaluation: building small test sets to measure prompt changes objectively, not by vibes
Prompting dynamo itself — since it's small (33M params), understanding what prompting even achieves at that scale vs a frontier LLM

topics.title: Embeddings + Vector DBs
topics.note: You've already built CLIP + FAISS semantic image search — this generalizes to text embeddings and production vector DBs beyond a local FAISS index.

subtopics.title: Text embedding models: sentence-transformers vs API-based embeddings (OpenAI/Cohere-style) — quality/cost tradeoffs
Beyond FAISS: managed/production vector DBs (Qdrant, Weaviate, pgvector) — when local FAISS stops being enough
resources: pgvector docs (fits naturally with your Postgres/Django stack)

subtopics.title: Indexing strategies: HNSW vs IVF vs flat — revisit your FAISS project's index choice with this framing
resources: FAISS wiki: 'Guidelines to choose an index'

subtopics.title: Chunking strategies for text (fixed-size, semantic, recursive) — foundational for RAG next
resources: LangChain/LlamaIndex docs on text splitters (concepts, not necessarily the framework)

subtopics.title: Hybrid search: combining vector similarity with keyword/BM25 for better retrieval
RAG fundamentals: retrieve-then-generate pipeline, why it beats pure fine-tuning for factual grounding
Build a from-scratch RAG pipeline (no framework) using pgvector/Qdrant + a Groq LLM call — fits your 'no black-box abstractions' style
Retrieval quality evaluation: recall@k, MRR, and why bad retrieval quietly ruins RAG output quality
resources: RAGAS docs (evaluation framework, concepts transferable even if you don't use the lib)

subtopics.title: Advanced RAG: re-ranking (cross-encoders), query rewriting/expansion, multi-hop retrieval
Agentic RAG: letting your multi-agent tool decide when/what to retrieve rather than always retrieving
resources: Self-directed, ties into your Groq multi-agent security tool

subtopics.title: Applied project: RAG over your own codebase/docs for the multi-agent coding tool's context retrieval

topics.title: Fine-tuning vs Prompting
topics.note: You have unique standing here — you've trained a model from scratch, so fine-tuning mechanics aren't new, but the decision framework and PEFT methods are.

subtopics.title: Decision framework: when prompting/RAG suffices vs when fine-tuning is actually justified (data availability, latency, cost)
Parameter-efficient fine-tuning: LoRA/QLoRA fundamentals — full fine-tuning is what you already know (from-scratch training), this is the lighter-weight alternative
Fine-tune a small open-weight model (or continue dynamo's training) with LoRA for a specific task, compare to prompting the same task
resources: Self-directed project, natural extension of dynamo

subtopics.title: Instruction tuning vs RLHF/DPO — conceptual overview of how far past your from-scratch pretraining these techniques go
Catastrophic forgetting and evaluation regressions after fine-tuning — how to detect them
resources: Survey papers on continual learning / catastrophic forgetting

topics.title: Goal Projects: Production AI systems combining everything above

subtopics.title: Deploy dynamo via TorchServe or a hand-rolled batching layer in FastAPI, with monitoring dashboards
Build a from-scratch RAG system (pgvector + Groq) over your own project docs, exposed through the dynamo-style API
Add agentic RAG + tool-calling to your multi-agent security-review coding tool, with prompt evals to measure reliability
LoRA fine-tune a small model for a narrow task and write up a fine-tuning-vs-prompting comparison as a portfolio piece
